skill.name: audio-transcriber
skill.description: Transcribes audio recordings into timestamped Markdown documents, with optional AI-generated summaries and meeting minutes.
skill.summary: Transcribes audio files into timestamped Markdown notes and optional AI summaries.
skill.author: Eric Andrade

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and credential patterns. Most markdown and changelog detections are false positives from documented examples, but executable scripts confirm high-risk behavior: transcript text can be sent to external AI CLIs, dependencies install automatically, and an example heredoc interpolates user-controlled filenames into Python code. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it should not publish until the executable risks and privacy disclosure are fixed.

high_findings.title: Transcript Disclosure to External AI CLIs
high_findings.description: The script builds a prompt from the full transcript and sends it to Claude CLI or GitHub Copilot CLI when a supported CLI is detected. This is expected for AI summaries, but it can expose sensitive meeting or interview content to external services and conflicts with the privacy-first messaging in the README.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly concatenates transcript text into full_prompt and passes it as input to external CLIs. The behavior is intentional but risky for sensitive audio and is not clearly separated from local-only transcription claims.

high_findings.title: User-Controlled Filename Interpolated Into Python Heredoc
high_findings.description: The example shell script expands AUDIO_FILE and MODEL directly inside an unquoted Python heredoc. A crafted filename can break the generated Python string and execute Python code when users run the example.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: AUDIO_FILE comes from the first command-line argument and is inserted into Python source without JSON encoding or argv passing. This is a concrete injection pattern in an executable example script.

medium_findings.title: Implicit Python Package Installation at Runtime
medium_findings.description: The main script attempts to install rich and tqdm with pip during import if they are missing. Automatic dependency installation changes the user environment and can download code before the user has reviewed dependencies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls execute pip install with fixed package names, so command injection is unlikely. The risk is supply-chain exposure and unapproved environment modification.

medium_findings.title: Installer Modifies System and Python Environments
medium_findings.description: The installation script runs pip installs, can use Homebrew to install ffmpeg, and can download a Whisper model. These actions are normal for this skill but require explicit user trust and isolation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are fixed and aligned with dependency setup, but they modify local tooling and may contact package or model registries. This is legitimate but elevated risk for marketplace users.

medium_findings.title: Audio Transcript Prompt Injection Surface
medium_findings.description: Audio-derived transcript text is included in prompts sent to AI CLIs for prompt generation and document creation. Malicious speech in a recording could influence the generated summary or meeting minutes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The transcript is untrusted content and is embedded directly into model prompts. I did not find tool execution from model output, so impact appears limited to output integrity and possible privacy instructions.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Mostly Static-Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command detections occur inside README, SKILL, CHANGELOG, and reference code fences. These are documentation examples rather than code executed by the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown prose or fenced examples. They explain usage and are not executed unless a user manually copies them.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Variable References Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The GCP, Azure, and AssemblyAI credential references appear in a comparison document that explains third-party tools. No evidence found that the skill reads or exfiltrates those credentials at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential names are shown in setup examples and sample client code. The executable scripts inspected do not use these cloud speech API keys.

low_findings.title: Sudo and Weak-Crypto Static Hits Are Not Confirmed Runtime Risks
low_findings.description: The sudo detections are printed Linux installation suggestions, and the weak-crypto detections appear to be triggered by unrelated documentation text. No evidence found of weak cryptographic operations in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install script only echoes sudo apt and yum commands instead of running them. I did not find executable hashing or encryption code at the cited markdown locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation With Sensitive Input
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run invokes Claude and GitHub Copilot CLIs with transcript-derived prompt input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The invocation is explicit and receives full_prompt as input. The main uncertainty is the exact privacy behavior of the installed CLI providers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Dependency Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script calls pip install from within the runtime path when optional UI packages are missing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and package names are static. The risk comes from network package installation during normal execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Shell-to-Python String Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: The example script injects shell variables into Python source instead of passing them as arguments or encoded data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in the heredoc. User-controlled AUDIO_FILE can affect generated Python syntax.

content.value_statement: Audio recordings are difficult to search, share, and convert into decisions. This skill creates timestamped Markdown transcripts and optional AI summaries for meetings, interviews, lectures, and recordings.
actual_capabilities: Detects Faster-Whisper or OpenAI Whisper for local audio transcription.
Creates timestamped Markdown transcript files from common audio formats.
Uses Claude CLI or GitHub Copilot CLI when available for optional summaries.
Supports custom prompts for meeting minutes, executive summaries, or structured notes.
Includes installation scripts and a manual shell example for dependency setup.
limitations: Requires Python, a Whisper engine, and enough local compute for transcription.
AI summaries may send transcript text to external CLI services when enabled.
Speaker diarization claims depend on the selected transcription engine and configuration.
Example scripts need review before use with untrusted filenames or shared systems.

use_cases.title: Create Meeting Records
use_cases.description: Convert recorded standups or planning meetings into Markdown transcripts, decisions, and action items.

use_cases.title: Document Interviews
use_cases.description: Turn interview recordings into timestamped notes that are easier to review, quote, and summarize.

use_cases.title: Summarize Lectures
use_cases.description: Generate structured notes from lectures, workshops, or training sessions for later study.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Transcript
prompt_templates.prompt: Transcribe this audio file into a timestamped Markdown document. Keep the wording close to the original speech.

prompt_templates.title: Meeting Minutes
prompt_templates.prompt: Transcribe this meeting and create Markdown minutes with participants, topics, decisions, and action items.

prompt_templates.title: Interview Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Transcribe this interview and summarize key themes, notable quotes, follow-up questions, and unresolved points.

prompt_templates.title: Executive Brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a concise executive brief from this audio. Include context, risks, decisions, owners, deadlines, and open questions.

output_examples.output: A Markdown transcript with timestamps, a meeting minutes section, decisions, and assigned action items.
A timestamped transcript with a summary of pain points, feature requests, and follow-up opportunities.

output_examples.input: A lecture recording in MP3 format.
output_examples.output: Structured study notes with topic sections, key explanations, and a short recap.

best_practices: Review privacy requirements before enabling Claude CLI or GitHub Copilot CLI summaries.
Use a virtual environment for Python dependencies and model downloads.
Test with a short audio sample before processing long or sensitive recordings.
anti_patterns: Do not run example scripts with filenames from untrusted users.
Do not process confidential audio with cloud AI summaries unless policy allows it.
Do not assume speaker labels are accurate without reviewing the transcript.

faq.question: Does this skill transcribe audio locally?
faq.answer: The Whisper transcription path runs locally after dependencies and models are installed.

faq.question: Can it create meeting minutes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use an available Claude or GitHub Copilot CLI to turn transcripts into structured minutes.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Initial dependency and model installation may require internet. AI summary CLIs may also contact external services.

faq.question: Which audio formats are supported?
faq.answer: The documentation lists common formats such as MP3, WAV, M4A, OGG, FLAC, and WEBM.

faq.question: Can I use a custom summary prompt?
faq.answer: Yes. The script accepts a custom prompt and can improve or replace it through the prompt workflow.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive recordings?
faq.answer: Use transcript-only mode for sensitive content. Optional AI summaries can disclose transcript text to external CLI providers.
